g.data-narration: Welcome to Pattern Classification! Today we explore how computers assign labels to objects based on their features.
Pattern classification assigns objects to predefined categories based on measurements called features. It's the foundation of computer vision.
g.text: The process of assigning a physical object or event to one of several
Example: Classifying an image as "cat", "dog", or "apple"

g.data-narration: Key terms: A pattern is a feature vector. A class is a category. Features are measurable properties. The classifier maps features to classes.
g.text: Class: Category or label (e.g., cat, dog)
Classifier: Maps feature vector to class
→ Creates a point in n-dimensional feature space

g.data-narration: The pipeline has four stages: image acquisition, preprocessing and segmentation, feature extraction, and final classification.
g.text: Noise removal, isolate ROI → Calculate descriptors → Compare to known patterns

g.data-narration: Classifiers define decision boundaries that separate regions in feature space. Each region belongs to a different class.
The Minimum Distance Classifier works on a simple principle: an unknown pattern belongs to the closest class prototype.
g.text: Core Principle: An unknown pattern belongs to the class whose
"Find the nearest neighbor prototype and adopt its class label"

g.data-narration: In training, we compute the prototype (mean vector) for each class by averaging all training patterns in that class.
g.text: Compute prototype (mean vector) for each class:
μ₁ = mean of all patterns in Class 1
μ₂ = mean of all patterns in Class 2
... and so on for C classes
where N = number of training samples
and xᵢ = each training feature vector

g.data-narration: For classification: calculate distance from unknown pattern to each prototype, then assign to the class with minimum distance.
g.text: 1. Calculate distance d(x, μᵢ) to each class prototype μᵢ
2. Assign x to class j where d(x, μⱼ) = min d(x, μᵢ)
d(x, μⱼ) = min{d(x, μ₁), d(x, μ₂), ...}

g.data-narration: Three common distance metrics: Euclidean (straight-line), Manhattan (grid-based), and Mahalanobis (accounts for correlations).
The decision boundary is a perpendicular bisector between prototypes. Regions are Voronoi cells - all points closer to one prototype.
g.text: Decision boundary = perpendicular bisector → Voronoi cells

g.data-narration: MDC is simple and fast but assumes spherical classes. It struggles with complex shapes and is sensitive to outliers in training data.
g.text: • Works well for compact, well-separated classes
• Sensitive to outliers in training data
• Poor if classes have different sizes

g.data-narration: Let's work through an example. Given two classes with training data, we'll compute prototypes and classify unknown pattern x = [4, 3].
Calculate Euclidean distances: d(x,μ₁)≈2.54 and d(x,μ₂)≈1.80. Since μ₂ is closer, assign x to Class 2.
g.text: Result: Assign x = [4,3] to Class 2 (ω₂) because 1.80 < 2.54

g.data-narration: MDC connects to morphology: morphological operations clean the image, feature extraction computes descriptors, then MDC classifies.
g.text: Erosion/Dilation clean ROI → Area, Perimeter, Compactness → Assign class based on nearest prototype

g.data-narration: In summary: pattern classification assigns labels using features. MDC is simple - find the nearest prototype. Know the pipeline, formulas, and limitations.
g.text: 1. Pattern Classification: Assign labels to objects based on extracted features
2. MDC Principle: Find nearest class prototype (mean vector)
3. Decision Rule: x → Class j if d(x, μⱼ) = min { d(x, μᵢ) }
4. Remember: Assumes spherical classes, sensitive to outliers
